title: Welsh cawl
subtitle: Lamb, leek, and root vegetable broth
excerpt: The national dish of Wales: lamb neck or shoulder simmered slowly with leeks, potatoes, swede, and carrots in a clear, golden broth. Served as a two-course meal with the broth first and the meat and vegetables after.
sourceNotes: Welsh cawl is the national dish of Wales, documented in Welsh culinary tradition from the mediaeval period. Bobby Freeman, First Catch Your Peacock: A Book of Welsh Food (1980), is the definitive modern account of the dish. The traditional version uses lamb or mutton with leeks, swede, and potatoes. The dish is also mentioned in historical records as a winter staple of Welsh farming households.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. The broth and vegetables freeze best without the leeks, which are added fresh when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Traditionally made in a large pot to last several days. Better the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made a day ahead. The fat can be lifted off after refrigerating overnight and the flavour deepens.

content.text: Welsh cawl is a soup and a stew at the same time, traditionally served as two courses: the clear, golden broth in bowls first, eaten with bread, and the lamb and vegetables served on plates after. Modern versions often serve both together. Either way is correct; the important thing is that the broth is properly made and well skimmed, and the lamb is cooked until it falls from the bone.

Leeks are essential and non-negotiable. They should go in for the last 20 minutes only; added earlier they collapse into the broth and lose their character. The traditional accompaniment is a piece of sharp Welsh cheese such as Caerphilly, crumbled into the broth.

items.prepNote: stalks tied for the pot, leaves chopped to finish

content.text: Place the lamb pieces in a large stockpot and cover with {{water}} of cold water. Bring to the boil and skim any grey foam thoroughly for the first 10 minutes. Add the onion, parsley stalks, thyme, and bay leaves. Season generously with salt. Partially cover and simmer very gently over low heat for 1.5 to 2 hours until the lamb is very tender and coming away from the bone.
Lift out the lamb pieces and set aside to cool slightly. Discard the onion, parsley stalks, thyme, and bay leaves. Remove the meat from the bones and return it to the broth in large pieces.

Bring the broth back to a simmer and add {{carrot}} carrot pieces, {{swede}} swede pieces, and {{potato}} potato pieces. Cook for 25 minutes until the vegetables are tender. Add {{leek}} sliced leeks for the last 20 minutes. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Scatter with the reserved chopped parsley leaves before serving.

To serve in the traditional manner, ladle the broth into bowls first and serve with bread. Follow with the meat and vegetables on plates. Crumbled Caerphilly over the broth is traditional and worth it.

items.cause: Insufficient skimming at the start, or the broth boiled rather than simmered
items.fix: Keep the heat low throughout. A clear broth is prized in cawl. If already cloudy, it does not affect the flavour.
Leeks should go in for no more than 20 minutes at the end. They should be soft but not collapsed.

items.symptom: Lamb is still tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Neck pieces were very large, or the heat was too high
items.fix: Continue at a very gentle simmer. Lamb neck on the bone will eventually yield at low heat; rushing it with higher heat makes it tougher.

items.symptom: Broth has a layer of fat on the surface
items.cause: Lamb neck is a fatty cut
items.fix: Make the cawl a day ahead and refrigerate overnight. The fat will solidify and can be lifted off easily. This is one reason cawl is traditionally made in advance.

content.text: Cawl is the national dish of Wales, made in some form since the mediaeval period and documented in its modern form in Bobby Freeman's First Catch Your Peacock (1980), the most thorough account of traditional Welsh cooking. The word means broth or soup in Welsh and has no precise English equivalent: it is both a cooking method and a dish. The two-course tradition of serving broth first and meat with vegetables second is practical in origin; it was the way to make a single pot of ingredients feed more people over more meals. Modern Welsh cooks often serve it combined in deep bowls, which is equally legitimate.
